questions.stem: Friedreich ataxia results from deficiency of frataxin, a nuclear-encoded mitochondrial protein. Which of the following best describes the normal function of frataxin?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Serving as a subunit of electron transport chain complex I

choices.label: C
choices.text: Acting as the electron carrier released to trigger apoptosis

choices.label: D
choices.text: Charging the mitochondrial tRNA for lysine

choices.label: E
choices.text: Mitochondrial iron metabolism and iron homeostasis

explanation.summary: Frataxin plays a crucial role in mitochondrial iron metabolism and iron homeostasis.
explanation.whyCorrect: Frataxin is a nuclear-encoded mitochondrial protein that plays a crucial role in mitochondrial iron metabolism and iron homeostasis. Deficient or non-functional frataxin, caused by FXN GAA repeat expansions, underlies Friedreich ataxia.

whyWrongByChoice.A: mtDNA repair is limited in mitochondria and is not the role of frataxin.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Complex I subunits are encoded by genes such as MT-ND1/MT-ND4 (implicated in LHON), not by frataxin.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Cytochrome C is the electron carrier released to trigger apoptosis, not frataxin.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The tRNA-lysine gene MT-TK relates to MERRF; frataxin does not charge tRNAs.

questions.stem: Mitochondria help determine whether a cell will undergo programmed cell death. Which of the following mitochondrial events triggers apoptosis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Accumulation of acetyl-CoA in the matrix

choices.label: B
choices.text: Release of cytochrome C from the mitochondrion

choices.label: C
choices.text: Fusion of two mitochondria into one

choices.label: D
choices.text: Import of nuclear-encoded proteins into the mitochondrion

explanation.summary: Release of cytochrome C from mitochondria triggers apoptosis.
explanation.whyCorrect: When a mitochondrion releases cytochrome C, normally an electron carrier of the electron transport chain, apoptosis (programmed cell death) is triggered and the cell dies. This makes mitochondria central regulators of whether a cell lives or dies.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Acetyl-CoA production is a normal metabolic function of mitochondria and does not by itself trigger apoptosis.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Mitochondrial fusion adjusts to the cell's energy needs and is not the trigger for programmed cell death.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Import of nuclear-encoded proteins is required for normal mitochondrial function, not a death signal.
whyWrongByChoice.E: mtDNA replication accompanies mitochondrial division and is unrelated to triggering apoptosis.

questions.stem: The mitochondrial genome contains only 37 genes, of which 13 are protein-encoding. All 13 protein-encoding genes encode subunits of which of the following?

explanation.summary: The 13 protein-coding mtDNA genes all encode oxidative phosphorylation subunits.
explanation.whyCorrect: mtDNA is a small circular genome of about 16,569 base pairs containing 37 genes: 13 protein-encoding (all oxidative phosphorylation subunits), 22 tRNA genes, and 2 rRNA genes. The remaining ~1100 mitochondrial proteins are nuclear-encoded and imported from the cytoplasm.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Glycolytic enzymes function in the cytoplasm and are not encoded by mtDNA.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Urea cycle enzymes are nuclear-encoded, not products of the 13 mtDNA protein-coding genes.
whyWrongByChoice.C: mtDNA has no histones, and histone proteins are nuclear-encoded.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Cytoplasmic ribosomal proteins are nuclear-encoded; mtDNA encodes only 2 rRNAs and 22 tRNAs for its own translation.

questions.stem: A 6-year-old girl is evaluated for obesity and difficulty walking. At birth she had poor muscle tone and difficulty feeding. She now has a voracious appetite, and her family has locked the refrigerator to control her intake. Her BMI is 39, and she has mild intellectual impairment and hypogonadism. Which of the following best describes the underlying genetic abnormality?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Loss of paternally expressed genes at the 15q11-q13 region

choices.label: B
choices.text: Loss of maternally expressed UBE3A at the 15q11-q13 region

choices.label: C
choices.text: Hypomethylation of the paternal ICR1 at 11p15

explanation.summary: Infantile hypotonia and feeding difficulty followed by childhood hyperphagia, obesity, and hypogonadism is the classic course of Prader-Willi syndrome, caused by loss of paternal gene expression at 15q11-q13.
explanation.whyCorrect: Prader-Willi syndrome results from loss of paternally expressed genes (including SNRPN) in the 15q11-q13 region, most often from paternal microdeletion or maternal uniparental disomy. Because the maternal copy of this region is normally imprinted (silenced), absence of the paternal contribution leaves the genes unexpressed, producing the characteristic biphasic feeding pattern and obesity.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Loss of maternal UBE3A at 15q11-q13 causes Angelman syndrome, which presents with severe developmental delay, ataxia, seizures, and a happy demeanor, not obesity and hyperphagia.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Hypomethylation of paternal ICR1 at 11p15 lowers IGF2 and causes Silver-Russell syndrome, a growth-restriction disorder, the opposite of this child's obesity.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Paternal uniparental disomy of chromosome 6 dysregulates PLAGL1 and causes transient neonatal diabetes, not Prader-Willi syndrome.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Trisomy 21 (Down syndrome) is an aneuploidy, not an imprinting disorder, and does not produce this hypotonia-then-hyperphagia course.

questions.stem: A 3-year-old boy has severe intellectual disability with essentially absent speech. He is noted to be unusually happy, laughs and smiles frequently, and displays hand-flapping movements. He has microcephaly, ataxia, and a history of seizures. Which gene is most likely responsible for his condition?

explanation.summary: Happy demeanor with frequent laughter, minimal speech, ataxia, microcephaly, and seizures is Angelman syndrome, caused by loss of maternally expressed UBE3A.
explanation.whyCorrect: Angelman syndrome is caused by loss of maternal expression of UBE3A in the 15q11-q13 region. UBE3A encodes a ubiquitin ligase responsible for protein degradation in neurons and is paternally imprinted, so only the maternal allele is expressed in the brain; loss of that allele produces the neurodevelopmental phenotype.

whyWrongByChoice.A: SNRPN loss of paternal expression causes Prader-Willi syndrome, characterized by hypotonia, hyperphagia, and obesity.
whyWrongByChoice.C: IGF2 dysregulation at 11p15 causes Silver-Russell syndrome (growth restriction) or Beckwith-Wiedemann syndrome (overgrowth), not this happy-puppet phenotype.
whyWrongByChoice.D: PLAGL1 at 6q24 is associated with transient neonatal diabetes mellitus.
whyWrongByChoice.E: ZFP57 is a methylation-sensitive transcription factor implicated in maintaining imprints and in cancer, not in Angelman syndrome.

questions.stem: A family has multiple members affected by Angelman syndrome. Pedigree analysis shows that unaffected fathers who carry a UBE3A mutation have affected children, whereas unaffected mothers who carry the same mutation do not have affected children. Which of the following best explains this inheritance pattern?

choices.label: A
choices.text: UBE3A is paternally imprinted, so only a maternally transmitted mutation causes disease

choices.label: B
choices.text: UBE3A is maternally imprinted, so only a paternally transmitted mutation causes disease

choices.label: C
choices.text: UBE3A follows classic autosomal dominant Mendelian inheritance

choices.label: E
choices.text: The phenotype depends on mitochondrial inheritance from the mother

explanation.summary: For UBE3A the paternal allele is imprinted (silenced) and the maternal allele is expressed, so a UBE3A mutation causes Angelman syndrome only when inherited from the mother.
explanation.whyCorrect: Imprinting disorders do not follow typical Mendelian inheritance; the phenotype appears only when the deficient allele is transmitted by the parent whose allele is normally expressed. Because UBE3A is paternally imprinted (maternally expressed), a mutation causes disease only when inherited from the mother, so an unaffected carrier father can transmit disease to his children.

whyWrongByChoice.B: This reverses the imprint. If UBE3A were maternally imprinted, paternal transmission would cause disease, which contradicts the pedigree.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Classic autosomal dominant inheritance would produce disease regardless of the transmitting parent's sex, which is not observed.
whyWrongByChoice.D: UBE3A is on chromosome 15, not the X chromosome, and the pattern is not X-linked recessive.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Mitochondrial inheritance would produce maternal transmission of disease, whereas here it is the carrier father who transmits the affected phenotype.

questions.stem: A 2-year-old boy has severe intrauterine and postnatal growth restriction with a relatively large-appearing head, a triangular face with prominent forehead, body asymmetry with one leg longer than the other, and clinodactyly of the fifth finger. Molecular testing points to an abnormality at chromosome 11p15. Which of the following is the most likely molecular defect?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Hypomethylation of the paternal ICR1, reducing IGF2 expression

choices.label: B
choices.text: Hypermethylation of the paternal 11p15 region, increasing IGF2 expression

explanation.summary: Severe growth restriction with a triangular face and body asymmetry is Silver-Russell syndrome, most often caused by hypomethylation of the paternal ICR1 at 11p15 that reduces IGF2.
explanation.whyCorrect: ICR1 is normally methylated only on the paternal allele near the paternally expressed IGF2 gene. Hypomethylation of the paternal ICR1 allows an insulator protein to bind and block enhancers from activating IGF2, reducing IGF2 (a growth factor) expression and producing the growth-restricted Silver-Russell phenotype.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Hypermethylation of paternal 11p15 increases IGF2 and causes Beckwith-Wiedemann syndrome, an overgrowth disorder, not growth restriction.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Paternal 15q11-q13 deletion causes Prader-Willi syndrome, not a growth-restriction disorder with triangular facies.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Maternal uniparental disomy of chromosome 15 causes Prader-Willi syndrome.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Duplication of 6q24 is associated with transient neonatal diabetes, not Silver-Russell syndrome.

questions.stem: A newborn is noted to have a very large tongue, an umbilical hernia, and episodes of hypoglycemia, and he is large for gestational age. Genetic evaluation reveals an epigenetic abnormality of the 11p15 region. Which of the following molecular findings is most consistent with this presentation?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Hypomethylation of the paternal ICR1 at 11p15

explanation.summary: Macroglossia, umbilical hernia, hypoglycemia, and overgrowth describe Beckwith-Wiedemann syndrome, which in a subset of patients arises from hypermethylation of the paternal 11p15 region.
explanation.whyCorrect: Beckwith-Wiedemann syndrome is an overgrowth syndrome; in about 5-10% of patients it is caused by hypermethylation of the 11p15 region on the paternal allele. This aberrant hypermethylation dysregulates the IGF2/H19 locus and drives excess growth, giving macroglossia, abdominal wall defects, and neonatal hypoglycemia.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Hypomethylation of paternal ICR1 reduces IGF2 and causes Silver-Russell syndrome, a growth-restriction disorder, not overgrowth.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Loss of maternal expression at 15q11-q13 causes Angelman syndrome, unrelated to macroglossia and overgrowth.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Loss of paternal expression at 15q11-q13 causes Prader-Willi syndrome, which features hypotonia and later obesity, not neonatal overgrowth with macroglossia.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Trinucleotide repeat expansion causes disorders such as fragile X or Huntington disease, not this imprinting-related overgrowth syndrome.

questions.stem: A full-term neonate is small for gestational age and develops hyperglycemia requiring insulin during the first weeks of life. The diabetes resolves spontaneously by 3 months of age. The child is at increased risk of developing type 2 diabetes later in life. Which of the following imprinted loci is most likely involved?

explanation.summary: Low birth weight with neonatal diabetes that resolves by about 3 months is transient neonatal diabetes mellitus, linked to dysregulation of PLAGL1 at the 6q24 locus.
explanation.whyCorrect: Transient neonatal diabetes is associated with paternal uniparental disomy of chromosome 6, duplication of 6q24, or hypomethylation of the 6q24 imprinting control region, all leading to dysregulation of PLAGL1. About 50% of cases resolve spontaneously after roughly 3 months, though these patients remain at higher risk of later type 2 diabetes.

whyWrongByChoice.B: UBE3A at 15q11-q13 is the Angelman syndrome gene and is not associated with neonatal diabetes.
whyWrongByChoice.C: SNRPN at 15q11-q13 is associated with Prader-Willi syndrome; the type 2 diabetes there appears with childhood obesity, not as transient neonatal diabetes.
whyWrongByChoice.D: IGF2 at 11p15 is linked to Silver-Russell and Beckwith-Wiedemann syndromes, not transient neonatal diabetes.
whyWrongByChoice.E: H19 at 11p15 is part of the IGF2/H19 imprinted domain related to 11p15 syndromes, not to the 6q24 transient neonatal diabetes locus.

questions.stem: A geneticist wants a single first-line test that can simultaneously detect abnormal DNA methylation, copy number variations, and uniparental disomy within known imprinted regions, and can indicate which parental allele is affected. Which of the following techniques is most appropriate?

explanation.summary: MS-MLPA is the most widely used first-line test for imprinting disorders because it detects abnormal methylation, CNVs, and UPD and, being probe-dependent, can indicate the affected parental allele.
explanation.whyCorrect: MS-MLPA detects abnormal DNA methylation patterns on selected regions, copy number variations, and uniparental disomy in known imprinted regions in a single assay. Because it is probe-dependent, it can distinguish which parental allele is affected, making it the preferred first-line diagnostic test.

whyWrongByChoice.B: aCGH identifies CNVs across the genome but does not provide information about parental origin or methylation status, so further analysis is required.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Whole-genome (and exome) sequencing is not well-suited for identifying imprinting disorders because it does not readily assess methylation or parental origin.
whyWrongByChoice.D: FISH can confirm a deletion but does not detect methylation abnormalities or uniparental disomy and does not distinguish parental origin.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Karyotyping detects large structural or numerical chromosomal changes but cannot detect the methylation defects or UPD central to imprinting disorders.

questions.stem: A researcher is studying how a specific cis-acting DNA sequence establishes parent-of-origin gene silencing at an imprinted locus. This sequence lies within a CpG island near a gene promoter. Which of the following molecular events at this region most directly leads to silencing of the associated gene?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Methylation of cytosine residues within the imprinting control region

choices.label: B
choices.text: A point mutation changing the coding sequence of the gene

choices.label: C
choices.text: Acetylation of histones opening the chromatin

choices.label: D
choices.text: Deletion of the entire chromosome arm

choices.label: E
choices.text: Insertion of a trinucleotide repeat in the coding region

explanation.summary: Imprinting control regions lie within CpG islands, and methylation of their cytosine residues silences the associated gene without changing the DNA sequence.
explanation.whyCorrect: Correct imprinted expression depends on an imprinting control region (ICR), often located within a CpG island near a promoter. CpG islands are usually unmethylated to allow expression; when the ICR becomes methylated at cytosine residues, the gene is silenced. This is an epigenetic mark that alters chromatin compaction rather than the DNA sequence.

whyWrongByChoice.B: A point mutation changes the DNA sequence and is not the epigenetic, parent-of-origin mechanism of imprinting-based silencing.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Histone acetylation opens chromatin and promotes expression rather than silencing.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Deletion of a chromosome arm is a large structural change affecting many genes, not the specific methylation-based silencing of an ICR.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Trinucleotide repeat insertion is the mechanism of repeat-expansion disorders, not of imprinting control region silencing.

questions.stem: During normal development, imprinting marks inherited by an embryo must be reset so that each new gamete carries sex-appropriate imprints. At which stage does the genome-wide erasure and re-establishment of imprinting marks primarily occur?

choices.label: A
choices.text: During gametogenesis in primordial germ cells

choices.label: C
choices.text: In terminally differentiated somatic cells throughout life

choices.label: E
choices.text: During each round of mitosis in adult tissues

explanation.summary: Imprints are erased and rewritten during gametogenesis in primordial germ cells, when new sex-specific imprints are established.
explanation.whyCorrect: During gametogenesis, primordial germ cells undergo genome-wide demethylation that erases existing imprints, and new maternal or paternal imprints are then established in a sex-specific manner in developing gametes. These new marks are preserved through fertilization even during the waves of genome-wide demethylation that follow.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Imprints are already established in the gametes before fertilization; implantation is not when erasure and rewriting occur.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Somatic cells maintain the imprints inherited from the zygote; they do not erase and rewrite them.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Imprint resetting occurs in germ cells during gametogenesis, not specifically at puberty.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Imprints are stably maintained through mitosis in adult tissues, not reset with each division.

questions.stem: A child with a clinical imprinting disorder is found to have inherited both copies of a chromosome from a single parent, with no deletion or point mutation identified. This abnormal gene dosage of imprinted genes explains the phenotype. Which of the following mechanisms best describes this finding?

choices.label: D
choices.text: Somatic mosaicism for a coding mutation

explanation.summary: Inheriting both copies of a chromosome from one parent is uniparental disomy, a major cause of imprinting syndromes.
explanation.whyCorrect: Uniparental disomy (UPD) occurs when both copies of a chromosome come from one parent, often as a rescue event after abnormal chromosome segregation would otherwise cause a lethal aneuploidy. For imprinted genes, UPD directly alters gene dosage of the parent-of-origin-expressed allele and leads to disease.

whyWrongByChoice.B: A balanced reciprocal translocation exchanges segments between chromosomes without loss or gain of material and does not describe inheriting both copies from one parent.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Viable trisomy involves three copies of a chromosome, not two copies from a single parent with normal total number.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Somatic mosaicism for a coding mutation involves a DNA sequence change in a subset of cells, not uniparental inheritance of a chromosome.
whyWrongByChoice.E: A Robertsonian fusion joins two acrocentric chromosomes and is a structural rearrangement, not the parent-of-origin dosage abnormality described.

questions.stem: Two infants each have a documented 15q11-q13 microdeletion. One has Prader-Willi syndrome and the other has Angelman syndrome. Beyond identifying the deletion, which additional test is most useful to determine which parental allele is affected and thereby distinguish the two disorders?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Microsatellite or SNP analysis of the child and parents

choices.label: D
choices.text: Sequencing of an unrelated housekeeping gene

explanation.summary: Because Prader-Willi and Angelman syndromes involve the same 15q11-q13 region but opposite parental alleles, parental-origin testing such as microsatellite or SNP analysis (or DNA methylation analysis) is needed to distinguish them.
explanation.whyCorrect: Microsatellite or SNP analysis compares the child's polymorphic markers to those of both parents, revealing the parental origin of the affected allele and detecting uniparental disomy. This distinguishes Prader-Willi (loss of paternal expression) from Angelman (loss of maternal expression), since the deletion alone does not indicate which parent's allele is involved.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Karyotyping, like the initial detection, shows the deletion but cannot determine parental origin of the affected allele.
whyWrongByChoice.C: A growth hormone level assesses one clinical consequence and does not establish the parental origin of the genetic defect.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Sequencing an unrelated housekeeping gene provides no information about the imprinted 15q11-q13 region or its parental origin.
whyWrongByChoice.E: A complete blood count is unrelated to identifying parental allele origin in an imprinting disorder.

questions.stem: A cancer researcher observes that in many tumors the IGF2 gene loses its normal imprinting, resulting in increased IGF2 expression, and that this loss of imprinting can be detected in blood samples as a potential biomarker. Which of the following best explains the molecular basis of IGF2 loss of imprinting in these cancers?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Hypermethylation of ICR1 leading to biallelic IGF2 expression

choices.label: B
choices.text: Complete demethylation of all CpG islands genome-wide

choices.label: C
choices.text: A frameshift mutation inactivating the IGF2 coding region

choices.label: E
choices.text: Loss of the maternal UBE3A allele

explanation.summary: IGF2 loss of imprinting in cancer is associated with hypermethylation of ICR1, which leads to increased (effectively biallelic) IGF2 expression and can serve as a biomarker of cancer risk.
explanation.whyCorrect: Loss of imprinting of IGF2 is associated with hypermethylation of ICR1 and leads to increased gene expression in the majority of cancers. Because the maternal allele normally uses unmethylated ICR1 as an insulator to keep IGF2 silent, hypermethylation abolishes this insulation and drives excess IGF2, contributing to tumorigenesis; because loss of imprinting is detectable in blood, it is a candidate biomarker.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Genome-wide demethylation of all CpG islands is not the specific ICR1 mechanism described and would not selectively increase IGF2 through loss of imprinting.
whyWrongByChoice.C: A frameshift inactivating IGF2 would decrease IGF2 function, whereas loss of imprinting increases IGF2 expression.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Deletion of 6q24 relates to transient neonatal diabetes (PLAGL1), not IGF2 loss of imprinting in cancer.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Loss of the maternal UBE3A allele causes Angelman syndrome and is unrelated to IGF2 expression in tumors.

questions.stem: A medical student is asked to classify the factors that contribute to a multifactorial (complex) disorder such as hypertension. Which of the following is LEAST characteristic of the way multifactorial disorders arise?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Epigenetic modifications such as DNA methylation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Environmental exposures such as diet and toxins

choices.label: E
choices.text: Lifestyle factors such as physical activity and stress

explanation.summary: Multifactorial disorders arise from many interacting factors rather than a single gene.
explanation.whyCorrect: By definition, multifactorial diseases do not follow a clear-cut, single-gene inheritance pattern; they result from the combined action of many genetic variants together with epigenetic, environmental, and lifestyle influences. Classic monogenic Mendelian inheritance is the exception, not the rule, for these conditions.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Genetic predispositions from multiple variants increase susceptibility and are a core component of multifactorial disease.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Epigenetic modifications such as CpG methylation play a crucial role in these disorders.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Environmental exposures including diet, stress, and toxins shape disease risk and progression.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Lifestyle factors are among the environmental influences that contribute significantly.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman whose father developed early-onset Alzheimer disease asks her physician about her own chance of developing the condition given this family history. The likelihood the physician estimates for her, because an affected relative is already known, is best described by which of the following terms?

explanation.summary: Recurrence risk is the likelihood of disease when a family member is already affected.
explanation.whyCorrect: The brick distinguishes occurrence risk (chance of disease with NO family history) from recurrence risk (chance of disease when someone in the family already has it). Because this woman has an affected father, the estimate applies specifically to recurrence risk.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Occurrence risk applies when there is no family history, which is not the case here.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Penetrance describes the proportion of genotype carriers who express a phenotype, not familial risk estimation.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Concordance rate compares affected status between twin pairs, not an individual's familial risk.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Population prevalence is the overall frequency in a population, independent of a specific family history.

questions.stem: Neural tube defects occur in approximately 1 in 1,500 live births, reflecting a relatively low population prevalence. A couple whose first child was born with a neural tube defect is told their recurrence risk in a future pregnancy is roughly 2-5%, about 37.5 times higher than the baseline. Using the liability-threshold model, which statement best explains this pattern?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A low liability threshold produces both low prevalence and low recurrence risk

choices.label: B
choices.text: The defect follows autosomal dominant inheritance with 50% recurrence

choices.label: C
choices.text: Recurrence risk is independent of how many genetic risk factors a family carries

choices.label: D
choices.text: A high liability threshold produces low prevalence but higher recurrence risk

choices.label: E
choices.text: A high threshold lowers recurrence risk because fewer relatives are susceptible

explanation.summary: A higher liability threshold lowers population prevalence yet raises recurrence risk in affected families.
explanation.whyCorrect: In the threshold model, a higher threshold means more genetic and environmental risk factors are required to cross into the affected range, so the disease is rare in the general population. But an affected individual must carry a heavy load of risk factors, and their relatives share more of those factors, giving a higher recurrence risk, as with the ~2-5% recurrence for neural tube defects.

whyWrongByChoice.A: A high (not low) threshold underlies the low prevalence, and recurrence risk within families is elevated, not low.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Neural tube defects are multifactorial, not simple autosomal dominant traits with 50% recurrence.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Recurrence risk rises with the genetic risk-factor load shared among relatives.
whyWrongByChoice.E: A high threshold increases recurrence risk in affected families because affected members carry more risk factors.

questions.stem: Pyloric stenosis is about four times more common in male infants than in female infants. A pediatric geneticist counsels the family of an affected female infant that the relatives of an affected girl carry a notably higher recurrence risk than the relatives of an affected boy. Which concept best accounts for this observation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The condition is X-linked recessive, sparing most females

choices.label: B
choices.text: Males have a higher threshold and therefore transmit more risk alleles

choices.label: C
choices.text: Females have a higher threshold, so an affected girl must carry a greater genetic load

choices.label: D
choices.text: Sex has no effect on the liability threshold for multifactorial traits

choices.label: E
choices.text: An affected female indicates the trait is purely environmental in her family

explanation.summary: When the less-susceptible sex is affected, it signals a heavier genetic load and higher recurrence risk in relatives.
explanation.whyCorrect: The brick explains that females have a higher threshold for pyloric stenosis, requiring more genetic and environmental risk factors. An affected female therefore carries a greater genetic load despite the higher threshold, and her relatives are more likely to inherit those risk factors, producing a higher familial recurrence risk (the Carter effect).

whyWrongByChoice.A: Pyloric stenosis is a multifactorial trait, not an X-linked recessive single-gene disorder.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Males have the LOWER threshold; the male predominance reflects easier crossing into the affected range.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The incidence of many multifactorial diseases differs by sex precisely because thresholds differ.
whyWrongByChoice.E: An affected female implies a heavier genetic contribution, not a purely environmental cause.

questions.stem: A study of oral clefts found a concordance rate of 50% in monozygotic twins compared with 8% in dizygotic twins. Which conclusion about oral clefts is best supported by these data?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It is a purely environmental condition with no genetic basis

choices.label: B
choices.text: It is a multifactorial condition with a substantial genetic component

choices.label: C
choices.text: It is a single-gene autosomal recessive disorder

choices.label: D
choices.text: Genes play no role because monozygotic twins are not 100% concordant

choices.label: E
choices.text: Environment plays no role because concordance is higher in monozygotic twins

explanation.summary: Higher MZ than DZ concordance, but well below 100%, indicates a multifactorial trait with a strong genetic component.
explanation.whyCorrect: Because monozygotic twins share nearly identical genotypes, concordance far above the dizygotic rate (50% vs 8%) points to a strong genetic contribution. Yet the fact that 50% of MZ twins are discordant shows non-genetic factors also matter, so oral cleft is multifactorial with a substantial genetic component.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The much higher MZ concordance demonstrates a clear genetic contribution, not a purely environmental cause.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Single-gene autosomal recessive inheritance would predict very different, more predictable concordance, not this multifactorial pattern.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Higher MZ than DZ concordance directly implicates genes; incomplete concordance does not erase that.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Incomplete MZ concordance (50% discordant) shows environmental factors do contribute.

questions.stem: Investigators note that a pair of monozygotic twins is discordant for a particular disease, meaning only one twin is affected despite the twins being genetically nearly identical. What does discordance in monozygotic twins most strongly suggest about the disease?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The disease is caused solely by a single dominant gene

choices.label: C
choices.text: The disease has complete genetic determination

choices.label: D
choices.text: The disease cannot be studied using twin methods

choices.label: E
choices.text: Non-genetic factors contribute to the disease

explanation.summary: Discordance in genetically identical twins points to non-genetic contributions and a multifactorial origin.
explanation.whyCorrect: Monozygotic twins have nearly identical genotypes, so if only one twin is affected, genetics alone cannot explain the difference. The brick states this discordance indicates that non-genetic factors contribute, supporting a multifactorial origin.

whyWrongByChoice.A: A single fully penetrant dominant gene would tend to make identical twins concordant, not discordant.
whyWrongByChoice.B: The premise specifies monozygotic (identical) twins; discordance does not reclassify their zygosity.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Complete genetic determination would predict concordance in identical twins, the opposite of what is seen.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Twin studies are in fact a powerful method for exactly this kind of gene-versus-environment analysis.

questions.stem: A research team wants to identify common genetic variants that occur more frequently in patients with type 2 diabetes than in unaffected controls, scanning the genomes of tens of thousands of individuals. Which study design is being described?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Linkage analysis in a single large family

explanation.summary: GWAS scan many genomes to find SNPs more common in cases than controls.
explanation.whyCorrect: A genome-wide association study scans the genomes of thousands to millions of individuals to find single-nucleotide polymorphisms that occur more frequently in people with a disease than in those without it. The brick notes GWAS have identified about 75 susceptibility loci for type 2 diabetes and related metabolic traits.

whyWrongByChoice.B: A randomized controlled trial tests interventions or treatments, not naturally occurring genetic associations.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Linkage analysis tracks co-inheritance within families and is suited to higher-penetrance, familial loci rather than common risk SNPs across populations.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Karyotype analysis detects large chromosomal abnormalities, not common single-nucleotide risk variants.
whyWrongByChoice.E: A twin concordance study estimates heritability, but it does not localize specific SNPs across the genome.

questions.stem: A geneticist explains that a typical human genome carries roughly 5 to 10 million single-nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs), most of which are benign and present in more than 1% of the population. Which term best describes this class of common, benign sequence variants?

explanation.summary: Polymorphisms are benign variants with a population frequency above 1%.
explanation.whyCorrect: The brick defines polymorphisms as benign mutations with a frequency greater than 1%, and notes a human genome typically carries 5 to 10 million SNPs. Some of these common variants are now recognized as risk factors for multifactorial disease through GWAS.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Deleterious mutations are rare variants with a direct disease-causing effect, especially in Mendelian disorders, not common benign variants.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Aneuploidies are gains or losses of whole chromosomes, not single-nucleotide variants.
whyWrongByChoice.D: De novo Mendelian mutations are rare, newly arising, disease-causing changes rather than common polymorphisms.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Frameshift mutations are a specific disruptive mutation type, not a description of common benign SNPs.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man is started on warfarin. Genotyping shows a CYP2C9 variant associated with reduced enzyme activity. Based on the pharmacogenomics discussed for warfarin, what is the most likely consequence of this variant, and how should dosing be adjusted?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Faster drug breakdown lowers warfarin levels, so a higher dose is needed

choices.label: B
choices.text: The variant has no effect on warfarin metabolism or dosing

choices.label: C
choices.text: The variant increases vitamin K synthesis, requiring more warfarin

choices.label: D
choices.text: Slower drug breakdown raises blood warfarin levels, so a lower dose is needed

choices.label: E
choices.text: The variant makes warfarin ineffective, so it should be discontinued entirely

explanation.summary: Reduced-activity CYP2C9 variants slow warfarin breakdown, raising drug levels and requiring lower doses.
explanation.whyCorrect: CYP2C9 encodes the liver enzyme that breaks down warfarin. Certain variants slow this process, increasing warfarin levels in the blood. The brick states patients with such variants may require lower doses to avoid bleeding, and this information is used clinically to personalize dosing.

whyWrongByChoice.A: A reduced-activity variant slows metabolism and raises levels; it does not speed breakdown or lower levels.
whyWrongByChoice.B: The variant meaningfully alters metabolism and is used to guide dosing, so it is not inconsequential.
whyWrongByChoice.C: CYP2C9 affects warfarin breakdown, not vitamin K synthesis; VKORC1 is the gene linked to the vitamin K epoxide reductase target.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Warfarin remains effective; dosing is simply reduced to prevent excessive anticoagulation.

questions.stem: GWAS have identified variants in VKORC1 that influence a patient's response to warfarin. According to the brick, VKORC1 affects warfarin response primarily through which mechanism?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Encoding the hepatic enzyme that metabolizes and clears warfarin

choices.label: C
choices.text: Altering expression of vitamin K epoxide reductase

choices.label: D
choices.text: Directly binding warfarin in the plasma to inactivate it

choices.label: E
choices.text: Encoding a serotonin receptor targeted by the drug

explanation.summary: VKORC1 alters expression of warfarin's target enzyme, vitamin K epoxide reductase, changing drug sensitivity.
explanation.whyCorrect: The brick states VKORC1 affects the drug's target enzyme by altering the expression of vitamin K epoxide reductase, which influences the body's sensitivity to warfarin. This is distinct from CYP2C9, which affects metabolism.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Metabolism and clearance of warfarin are governed by CYP2C9, not VKORC1.
whyWrongByChoice.B: The brick attributes VKORC1's effect to the target enzyme, not to intestinal absorption.
whyWrongByChoice.D: VKORC1 does not act by directly binding and inactivating warfarin in plasma.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Serotonin receptors (for example HTR1F) relate to migraine drug targets, not warfarin's mechanism.

questions.stem: A recent migraine GWAS discovered new risk loci, some containing genes that encode existing migraine-specific drug targets. Monoclonal antibodies and gepants used for migraine act on the product of which of these genes?

choices.label: C
choices.text: IL10RA, encoding an interleukin-10 receptor subunit

choices.label: E
choices.text: VKORC1, encoding vitamin K epoxide reductase

explanation.summary: Gepants and anti-CGRP monoclonal antibodies target CGRP, encoded by CALCA/CALCB.
explanation.whyCorrect: The brick notes that migraine GWAS identified loci with genes encoding migraine-specific drug targets, namely the calcitonin gene-related peptide (CALCA/CALCB). CGRP is the target of preventive and acute migraine therapies delivered via monoclonal antibodies and gepants.

whyWrongByChoice.B: CYP2C9 relates to warfarin metabolism, not migraine therapy.
whyWrongByChoice.C: IL10RA is implicated in inflammatory bowel disease, not migraine drug targeting.
whyWrongByChoice.D: HIST1H2BD is associated with cocaine dependence, not migraine treatment.
whyWrongByChoice.E: VKORC1 is a warfarin-response gene, unrelated to CGRP-targeting migraine drugs.

questions.stem: Studies suggest that a grandmother's smoking during pregnancy may alter DNA methylation patterns in her daughter and potentially in her grandchildren, influencing health outcomes beyond the immediate generation. This phenomenon is best described by which term?

explanation.summary: Environmental exposures altering methylation across generations reflect transgenerational epigenetic inheritance.
explanation.whyCorrect: The brick describes how prenatal environmental exposures, such as grandmaternal smoking, can alter DNA methylation and affect biological outcomes in later generations, a phenomenon called transgenerational epigenetic inheritance. Watkins et al. reported sex-specific methylation associations linked to grandmaternal smoking.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The example involves inherited methylation changes across generations, not a reversal of genomic imprinting.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Somatic mosaicism refers to genetically distinct cell populations within one individual, not inherited epigenetic marks.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Anticipation describes worsening or earlier onset over generations in repeat-expansion disorders, not methylation transmission.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Codominance is a Mendelian allelic expression pattern, unrelated to environmentally induced epigenetic changes.

questions.stem: A group of 500 people leaves an island by raft to settle a new island. Before departure, 400 have curly hair (dominant) and 100 have straight hair (recessive). During the voyage several rafts sink, and the group that arrives contains 375 with curly hair and 25 with straight hair. The change in allele proportions in the arriving group was caused by the random sinking of rafts rather than any survival advantage of a trait. Which phenomenon best explains this change in allele frequency?

explanation.summary: A random disaster that sharply cuts population size and shifts allele frequencies by chance is the bottleneck effect.
explanation.whyCorrect: The bottleneck effect is a sharp reduction in population size (and therefore alleles) caused by a random event such as a disaster. The sinking rafts randomly eliminated individuals, changing allele frequencies through genetic drift rather than through any fitness advantage.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Assortative mating is a preference for mating with similar individuals, which does not apply to a disaster that randomly removes people.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Natural selection favors alleles that confer a reproductive advantage; here the loss was random, with no trait providing an advantage.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Gene flow is the transfer of alleles between populations by migration and would increase variation, not the random loss seen here.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Mendelian inheritance describes random reassortment of parental alleles into offspring genotypes, not a population-level frequency shift from a disaster.

questions.stem: In a stable, randomly mating population of 10,000 people, 5 individuals are affected by an autosomal recessive condition. Assuming Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium, which of the following best approximates the frequency of the recessive allele (q)?

explanation.summary: The recessive allele frequency q is the square root of the homozygous recessive frequency q-squared.
explanation.whyCorrect: The affected homozygous recessive genotype frequency is q-squared = 5/10,000 = 0.0005. Taking the square root gives q = the square root of 0.0005, which is approximately 0.022.

whyWrongByChoice.A: 0.0005 is q-squared (the frequency of affected individuals), not the allele frequency q itself; you must take its square root.
whyWrongByChoice.B: 0.5 would only occur if both alleles were equally common, which is not the case for a rare disease.
whyWrongByChoice.C: 0.043 corresponds to the heterozygote frequency 2pq, not the recessive allele frequency.
whyWrongByChoice.E: 0.978 is the frequency of the dominant allele p (equal to 1 minus q), not the recessive allele frequency.

questions.stem: In a randomly mating population of 1,000 people at Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium, 20 people are affected by an autosomal recessive disease. Approximately how many people in this population are unaffected heterozygous carriers?

explanation.summary: Carrier count equals the heterozygote frequency 2pq multiplied by the total population.
explanation.whyCorrect: q-squared = 20/1000 = 0.02, so q = approximately 0.14 and p = 1 minus 0.14 = 0.86. The carrier frequency is 2pq = 2 x 0.86 x 0.14 = approximately 0.24, and 0.24 x 1000 = 240 carriers.

whyWrongByChoice.A: 24 results from multiplying the carrier frequency of 0.24 by 100 instead of by the true population of 1,000.
whyWrongByChoice.B: 140 confuses the allele frequency q (0.14) scaled to the population with the number of carriers, which requires 2pq.
whyWrongByChoice.D: 320 is not obtained by any correct step of the Hardy-Weinberg calculation.
whyWrongByChoice.E: 860 corresponds to p x 1000, the scaled dominant allele frequency, not the heterozygote count.

questions.stem: A woman with curly hair reports that she is only romantically attracted to men who also have curly hair. If this preference were widespread, it would reduce random pairing in the population. This behavior is an example of which genetic phenomenon?

explanation.summary: Preferring mates with similar traits, decreasing random mating, is assortative mating.
explanation.whyCorrect: Assortative mating refers to the tendency of individuals to mate with others who are similar to them, which decreases random mating within a population. Attraction to a partner who shares the same phenotype (curly hair) is phenotypic assortative mating, a violation of the Hardy-Weinberg random-mating assumption.

whyWrongByChoice.B: The bottleneck effect is a disaster-driven reduction in population size, not a mate-choice pattern.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Genetic drift is random change in allele frequency by chance and does not involve mate preference.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Gene flow is the transfer of alleles between populations by migration, unrelated to choosing similar mates.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The founder effect involves a small group separating from a larger population, not a mating preference.

questions.stem: The Afrikaner population of South Africa descends largely from a small number of Dutch colonists and today has an unusually high frequency of the gene that causes Huntington disease. This elevated frequency is best explained by which mechanism?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A recent environmental disaster reducing the population

choices.label: B
choices.text: Natural selection favoring the Huntington allele

choices.label: E
choices.text: A high spontaneous mutation rate at the Huntington locus

explanation.summary: A disease that is common because a few original settlers happened to carry the allele reflects the founder effect.
explanation.whyCorrect: The founder effect occurs when a small group separates from a larger population; the new subpopulation resembles that small founding group. Because the founding Dutch colonists carried the Huntington allele by chance, it spread to their descendants within the isolated community, raising its frequency.

whyWrongByChoice.A: A sudden disaster describes the bottleneck effect; the Afrikaner example is driven by a small founding group, not a catastrophe.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Huntington disease confers no reproductive advantage, so selection does not explain its high frequency; the spread was by chance.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Gene flow mixes alleles between populations and increases diversity; here an isolated community concentrated a founder allele.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The high frequency reflects inheritance from founders who already carried the allele, not an elevated mutation rate.

questions.stem: A physician in a small town notes that residents were historically descendants of southern European immigrants, among whom thalassemia minor was common. Over recent generations, young people left for university, married partners from elsewhere, and returned to raise families, so many current residents no longer share the southern European ancestry. The physician now observes that the incidence of thalassemia minor has decreased. Which mechanism best explains this decline?

choices.label: D
choices.text: Natural selection against the thalassemia allele

explanation.summary: New alleles entering a population through migration and mating is gene flow, which increases variation and dilutes a previously common allele.
explanation.whyCorrect: Gene flow is genetic variation caused by migration of individuals from other populations into a given population. As residents married outsiders and introduced new alleles, the shared inherited thalassemia allele became diluted, lowering the disease incidence. This may make broad screening less cost-effective than before.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The founder effect concentrates alleles in a small isolated group, the opposite of what mixing produces.
whyWrongByChoice.B: The bottleneck effect reduces population size via disaster and decreases diversity; here diversity is increasing through migration.
whyWrongByChoice.D: There is no described selective pressure acting against the thalassemia allele; the change is due to incoming alleles.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Assortative mating is preference for similar partners; the scenario describes mixing with dissimilar outsiders.

questions.stem: In regions of the world where malaria is endemic, the recessive HbS allele of the beta-globin chain is maintained at a relatively high frequency in the population, whereas in non-malarial regions it is selected against. Which of the following best explains why HbS persists where malaria is common?

choices.label: A
choices.text: HbS/HbA heterozygotes have a survival advantage against severe malaria

choices.label: B
choices.text: HbS/HbS homozygotes are protected from malaria and reproduce more

choices.label: C
choices.text: The HbS allele mutates back to HbA more slowly in malarial regions

choices.label: D
choices.text: Gene flow continually imports HbS from non-malarial regions

choices.label: E
choices.text: Random genetic drift fixes HbS in large malarial populations

explanation.summary: Heterozygote advantage: carriers with sickle cell trait resist the worst complications of malaria and reproduce, keeping HbS frequent.
explanation.whyCorrect: Carriers of HbS (genotype HbA/HbS) have sickle cell trait and are more likely to survive severe malaria, giving them a reproductive advantage where malaria is common. This natural selection for heterozygotes maintains the HbS allele in the population, even though homozygotes (HbS/HbS) have sickle cell disease.

whyWrongByChoice.B: HbS/HbS homozygotes have sickle cell disease and historically suffered early mortality, so they do not drive the allele's persistence.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Persistence is explained by selection favoring heterozygotes, not by differences in reverse-mutation rates.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Non-malarial regions select against HbS and have lower frequencies, so they are not a source of imported HbS.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Genetic drift is random and strongest in small populations; the pattern here is driven by consistent selection tied to malaria.

questions.stem: A 6-month-old infant of Ashkenazi Jewish descent presents with seizures, progressive neurologic deterioration, and a cherry-red macula on fundoscopic examination. This disorder results from deficiency of which of the following enzymes?

explanation.summary: Tay-Sachs disease results from hexosaminidase A deficiency and is more common in Ashkenazi Jews.
explanation.whyCorrect: Tay-Sachs disease results from a deficiency of the hexosaminidase A enzyme, causing seizures, a cherry-red macula, and other neurologic deficits. It is found at higher rates in Ashkenazi Jews, prompting ACOG to recommend screening for Ashkenazi Jews or those of mixed Jewish ancestry considering children.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Alpha-galactosidase A deficiency causes Fabry disease, which is not associated with this presentation in the brick.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Glucocerebrosidase deficiency causes Gaucher disease, another founder-effect disorder in Jewish populations, but it does not produce the cherry-red macula of Tay-Sachs.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The CFTR chloride transporter is defective in cystic fibrosis, not in this neurodegenerative presentation.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Phenylalanine hydroxylase deficiency causes phenylketonuria, which is not the disorder described here.

questions.stem: A genetic counselor explains to a couple that a cystic fibrosis carrier screening panel has an estimated sensitivity of 71.9% in white-American couples but only 23.4% in Asian-American couples. What is the best interpretation of this difference for counseling?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Asian-American couples have a higher absolute risk of having an affected child regardless of screening

choices.label: B
choices.text: A negative screen more reliably rules out carrier status in white-American than in Asian-American couples

choices.label: C
choices.text: The CFTR gene is present only in white-American populations

choices.label: D
choices.text: A positive screen is a false positive more often in white-American couples

choices.label: E
choices.text: Screening sensitivity is identical across ancestries once mutation counts are equalized

explanation.summary: Because causative CFTR mutations differ by ancestry, panel sensitivity varies, so a negative result is more trustworthy where sensitivity is higher.
explanation.whyCorrect: Carrier rates and the specific CFTR mutations causing cystic fibrosis are highly dependent on ancestry. Because the panel detects more of the relevant mutations in white-American couples (71.9% sensitivity) than in Asian-American couples (23.4%), a negative test is more likely to be truly negative for a white-American couple.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Lower sensitivity means residual risk is harder to exclude after a negative test; it does not by itself establish a higher baseline disease risk.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The CFTR gene is present in all populations; only the frequency and type of mutations differ.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The figures describe sensitivity (detection of true carriers), not the false-positive rate of a positive result.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The point of the example is that sensitivity genuinely differs by ancestry because causative mutations differ.

questions.stem: A textbook describes the differences in DNA sequence for a specific gene at the same position on a chromosome, noting that these differences can lead to diverse traits, disease risks, and medication responses within a population. This description best defines which term?

explanation.summary: Different alleles of a gene at the same locus contributing to varied phenotypes is allelic variation.
explanation.whyCorrect: Allelic variation describes differences in DNA sequence (alleles) for a specific gene at the same position on a chromosome. This variation contributes to observable phenotypes in both normal and pathogenic ways and increases overall genetic diversity, influencing traits, disease risk, and drug response.

whyWrongByChoice.B: The bottleneck effect is a type of genetic drift that decreases diversity, not the definition of sequence differences at a locus.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The founder effect is also a form of genetic drift and concentrates alleles in a small group; it is not the definition of allelic differences.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Genetic drift is random change in allele frequency and decreases diversity, rather than defining the differences themselves.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Assortative mating is a mate-choice pattern, unrelated to defining DNA sequence differences at a locus.

questions.stem: A student lists conditions under which a population would remain in Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium. Which of the following, if present in a population, would violate a Hardy-Weinberg assumption and shift allele frequencies over generations?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Absence of any immigration or emigration

explanation.summary: Selection favoring an allele violates the no-selection assumption of Hardy-Weinberg and changes allele frequencies.
explanation.whyCorrect: Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium assumes no selective forces favor one allele over another. If natural selection gives one allele a reproductive advantage, its frequency will change across generations, breaking equilibrium.

whyWrongByChoice.A: No immigration or emigration is a required assumption; its presence supports equilibrium.
whyWrongByChoice.B: A large population is one of the required assumptions; it helps maintain, not violate, equilibrium.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Random mating is an assumption of the model and supports equilibrium rather than disrupting it.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Absence of mutation is a required assumption; it preserves stable allele frequencies.

questions.stem: A population geneticist explains that a certain deleterious recessive allele persists in a population at a stable low frequency because the rate at which it is newly created by mutation is balanced by the rate at which it is removed by selection. This steady state is best described as which of the following?

explanation.summary: A balance between mutation creating deleterious alleles and selection removing them is mutation-selection equilibrium.
explanation.whyCorrect: The mutation-selection equilibrium is a state where the rate of creation of deleterious alleles (usually recessive) by mutation is balanced by the rate of their elimination by selection. This balance helps maintain genetic variation within populations at a stable low frequency.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The founder effect concentrates alleles in a small separated group and is a form of genetic drift, not a mutation-selection balance.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Gene flow is the migration-driven transfer of alleles between populations, not a within-population balance of mutation and selection.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The bottleneck effect is a disaster-driven reduction in population size, unrelated to a steady mutation-selection balance.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Assortative mating is a preference for similar mates and does not describe this equilibrium.
